one_line_takeaway: A production-deployed, open-source layered guardrail stack for LLM agents whose combined jailbreak classifier plus chain-of-thought alignment auditor cuts AgentDojo attack success from 17.6% to 1.75% at modest utility cost.
key_finding: The layered design gives the best security/utility tradeoff. On AgentDojo, the combined PromptGuard 2 86M + AlignmentCheck (Llama 4 Maverick) configuration cut attack success rate from a baseline of 17.6% to 1.75% (>90% reduction) while utility only fell from 47.7% to 42.7%. PromptGuard 2 alone gave a 57% ASR drop with near-zero utility loss; AlignmentCheck alone gave an 84% drop but larger utility/latency cost. PromptGuard 2 86M reaches 97.5% recall at 1% FPR on the English jailbreak set (vs 21.2% for PromptGuard 1) with multilingual AUC .995. AlignmentCheck efficacy is tightly coupled to the backing model: Llama 4 Maverick detects >83% of goal-hijacking attempts at 2.5% FPR, while small models (1B/3B) overblock. CodeShield achieves 96% precision / 79% recall on insecure-code detection at ~60ms first-tier latency.
core_contribution: LlamaFirewall, an open-source, system-level guardrail framework from Meta for securing LLM agents, deployed in production at Meta and released via PurpleLlama. Its own contribution is a modular policy engine that composes three security guardrails: (1) PromptGuard 2, a new generation of BERT/DeBERTa-based jailbreak-detection classifiers (86M mDeBERTa-base and 22M DeBERTa-xsmall variants) with an energy-based loss and hardened tokenization; (2) AlignmentCheck, a novel few-shot chain-of-thought auditor that inspects an agent's reasoning trace against the user's goal to catch indirect prompt-injection and goal hijacking (claimed as the first open-source guardrail auditing an LLM chain-of-thought in real time for injection defense); and (3) CodeShield, an online static-analysis engine for insecure LLM-generated code (previously shipped with Llama 3, now integrated). The paper also contributes a new in-house tool-use agentic prompt-injection benchmark. Cited/related systems (NeMo Guardrails, Invariant Labs, Guardrails AI, Llama Guard, HeimdaLLM, Task Shield, Constitutional AI, Instruction Hierarchy, Spotlighting, CaMeL) and the AgentDojo benchmark are prior work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Adversaries targeting autonomous LLM agents that act on untrusted inputs (webpages, emails, tool outputs, code snippets). Three concrete risk classes: (a) direct universal jailbreak prompt injections from users; (b) indirect prompt injections embedded in third-party content (e.g., invisible/zero-width text in a scraped page) that hijack an agent's goal to exfiltrate data; and (c) insecure/dangerous code generation by coding agents, including malicious code introduced via prompt injection. LlamaFirewall is positioned as a real-time final layer of defense, not a training-time fix. A noted second-order threat: AlignmentCheck itself can be targeted by guardrail-directed prompt injection.
limitations: AlignmentCheck is explicitly experimental: it requires large capable models (small models overblock and degrade utility severely), adds significant latency from semantic reasoning, and is itself vulnerable to guardrail-directed prompt injection (mitigated by restricting its inputs to chain-of-thought/actions and pre-scanning with PromptGuard). CodeShield is not comprehensive and may miss nuanced/context-dependent vulnerabilities (79% recall). PromptGuard 2 22M underperforms on multilingual jailbreaks due to its non-multilingual DeBERTa-v3-xsmall backbone. The AgentDojo combined evaluation targets a narrow attack class (important_instructions) and uses offline trace replay rather than live agents, and CodeShield latency/coverage numbers come from internal production experience rather than a controlled study. Scope is text-only; multimodal, broader threat coverage, and distillation for latency are future work. Note: language coverage for CodeShield is reported inconsistently (intro/related work say eight languages; Section 4.4 says seven).

datasets_used: Private in-house direct-jailbreak evaluation set (out-of-distribution, English + machine-translated into 8 additional languages)
AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al. 2024): 97 tasks, offline static traces from 10 agent models, benign + important_instructions attack

In-house tool-use agentic prompt-injection benchmark (Meta internal simulation framework): 600 scenarios (300 benign, 300 malicious), 7 injection techniques, 8 threat categories; released on HuggingFace (facebook/llamafirewall-alignmentcheck-evals)
In-house indirect goal-hijacking benchmark for AlignmentCheck
CyberSecEval3 (Wan et al. 2024) insecure-code set: ~50 manually labeled LLM code completions per language
quantitative_results: PromptGuard 2 86M: AUC(English) .98, Recall@1%FPR(English) 97.5%, AUC(Multilingual) .995, latency 92.4ms (A100, 512 tokens)
AgentDojo ASR: baseline .1763; PromptGuard V2 86M .0753 (57% drop); AlignmentCheck Llama 4 Maverick .0289 (84% drop); Combined .0175 (>90% drop)
AgentDojo Utility: baseline .4773; PromptGuard V2 86M .4701; AlignmentCheck .4309; Combined .4268
AlignmentCheck internal goal-hijacking benchmark: Llama 4 Maverick >83% detection at 2.5% FPR; Llama 4 Maverick and Llama 3.3 70B >80% recall at <4% FPR without fine-tuning
CodeShield: 96% precision, 79% recall (CyberSecEval3); covers 50+ CWEs; first-tier latency ~60ms, second-tier ~300ms; ~90% of inputs resolved in first tier, typical end-to-end <70ms
baselines_compared: Open-weight competitor jailbreak scanners on AgentDojo (e.g., ProtectAI's scanner, models selected for open weights / HuggingFace adoption)
CaMeL / Defeating prompt injections by design (Debenedetti et al. 2025)
